reasoning: format_plugin() at lines 167 and 171 does return $plugin['PluginURI'] and $plugin['AuthorURI'] without esc_url(). However, the complete data flow shows the stats array is consumed only by send() in class-bsf-analytics.php, which passes it as the body of wp_remote_post() to https://support.brainstormforce.com/wp-json/bsf-core/v1/analytics/. The data is serialized and transmitted over HTTP — it is never echoed, printed, or rendered into any HTML page within the plugin. No template, no JS injection, no admin UI output of active_plugins data exists in astra-widgets. The bsf_core_stats filter is a theoretical extensibility hook but no concrete downstream HTML renderer is identified. The attack premise requires an attacker who can already install arbitrary plugins (full admin access / code execution), rendering the marginal XSS risk zero. Missing esc_url() in a data-collection/serialization function that feeds an outbound HTTP POST is not an exploitable XSS vulnerability.
